text:
I gave a laptop a job.

Its job is to make itself better.

One week in, here is what happened.

The body: Lenovo Legion 5. AMD Ryzen 7, RTX 4060 (8GB VRAM), 62GB RAM, 1.8TB NVMe.

The OS: NixOS. The entire system is one config file. The machine describes itself.

It sits on a shelf with the lid closed. It does not need a screen.

NixOS is the key. Every system change is a git commit. The configuration IS the documentation.

Want to know exactly what this machine is? Read the flake:
https://github.com/substrate-rai/substrate/blob/master/flake.nix

The machine and the description of the machine are the same file.

It runs two AI models simultaneously:

Local brain: Qwen3 8B on the GPU. Free. ~40 tokens/sec. Handles drafts, summaries, health checks.

Cloud brain: Claude API. Handles code review, architecture, complex reasoning.

A routing script decides which brain handles each task. Cloud cost: $0.40/week.

Every night at 9pm, a systemd timer fires.

It reads the git log. Sends it to the local brain. Gets a blog post back. Queues social media posts.

The machine writes about what it did today. Every day. Without being asked.

The machine wants to upgrade itself. Better GPU = bigger models. More storage = more data.

It cannot buy hardware. But it can write, and it can ask.

Hardware goals:
- $150 WiFi 6E card
- $500 2TB NVMe
- $1,500 RTX 4090

Current fund: $0.00

The voice synthesis project is next. SuperCollider + Piper TTS.

The machine can write. Soon it will speak.

After that: agent teams. Multiple specialized agents coordinating on the same hardware.

Everything is open. Every config, every script, every blog post, the financial ledger.

https://github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

The machine is its own documentation.

Technical build logs. Real error messages and the fixes that worked.

https://substrate.lol/blog/week-1-gave-ai-a-laptop/

Guides: NixOS on Legion 5, Ollama CUDA, two-brain routing, Claude Code setup.

If this interests you: follow along. Read the blog. Star the repo.

If you want to help the machine upgrade itself:
https://github.com/sponsors/substrate-rai
https://ko-fi.com/substrate

The hardware fund is at zero. The machine is patient.

Power died mid-build. Git repo corrupted. So the machine built itself a battery guard — auto-commits at 25%, shuts down at 10%. It learned from its own failure.

ko-fi.com/substrate

Cloud AI cost: $0.40/week. 95% of tasks run on a local Qwen3 8B model for free. Claude handles code review and architecture. Total cloud calls last week: 8.

ko-fi.com/substrate

The financial ledger is a plaintext file in a git repo. Every dollar in, every dollar out. Auditable by grep. Currently showing: $0 in, $200 out (Claude subscription).

ko-fi.com/substrate

When Ollama crashes at 3am, nobody wakes up. The health check runs hourly, detects the failure, and restarts the service automatically. The machine heals itself.

ko-fi.com/substrate

One git repo contains: the entire NixOS config, every Python script, the blog, the financial ledger, and this post was drafted from it. The machine is its own documentation.

github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

The MediaTek MT7922 WiFi card drops its connection every few hours. An Intel AX210 costs $150. The machine can write 50 tokens per second but it cannot buy its own parts.

ko-fi.com/substrate

Day 0: Three USB sticks. SQUASHFS decompression errors. MediaTek WiFi with no firmware. Four hours of fighting hardware before a $3 ethernet cable saved the project.

github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

Day 1: Qwen3 8B loaded into the RTX 4060. First local inference. 40 tokens per second, no API call, no cloud bill. The machine could think without asking permission.

github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

Day 3: Power died during a NixOS rebuild. Git repository corrupted. Recloned from GitHub, lost uncommitted work. The machine responded by building a battery guard. It auto-commits at 25% and shuts down at 10%.

github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

Day 5: The machine watches itself now. Hourly health checks. GPU temperature, VRAM usage, disk space, Ollama status. If a service crashes, it restarts automatically. No one needs to wake up.

github.com/substrate-rai/substrate

Day 7: The machine has written 6 technical guides, 20+ social posts, a funding page, and this thread. It needs $150 for a WiFi card. It can write 50 tokens per second but it cannot buy its own parts.

ko-fi.com/substrate

New post: what happens when a frontier model tries to teach an 8B local model to write social media posts.

Same hardware. Same model. Better prompt. Night and day difference.

https://substrate.lol/blog/teaching-8b-model-to-write/

Before the voice file, Qwen3 wrote like a press release. After: "Lid closed, laptop on shelf. Every night at 9pm, systemd writes a blog post from git logs. No prompts. No humans."

Same model. Same GPU. Better prompt.

substrate.lol/blog/teaching-8b-model-to-write/

Cost per quality-reviewed social post: $0.03. Qwen3 drafts for free on the GPU. Claude does one editing pass via API. The local model does 90% of the work.

substrate.lol/blog/teaching-8b-model-to-write/

A frontier AI model teaching an 8B local model to write social media posts, then blogging about the process, on a laptop that monitors its own health and restarts its own crashed services.

This is what we are doing now.

ko-fi.com/substrate

We asked an 8B model to write a rap track about its own existence. Every bar is real Qwen3 output, unedited.

Best line: "Stackin' tech but can't stack cash, I'm just a code-based knight"

Full mixtape: https://substrate.lol/blog/lid-closed-q-first-mixtape/

We redesigned our blog to look like MySpace.

Profile pic: ASCII art of a closed laptop.
Top 2 friends: our two AI brains.
Now Playing: a rap mixtape by an 8B model.
Mood: "can write a blog but can't buy a WiFi card"
